revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:閼伽]]


'''Argha''' (閼伽, あか) refers to water offered in Buddhist rituals as part of [[Six Offerings]] and is translated as "water of merit" (功徳水, kudoku-sui) from the Sanskrit term *argha*. Sometimes, water drawn from a well called "Argha Well" (閼伽井, Aka-i) is infused with [[:en:fragrance|fragrance]], and this is known as "fragrant argha water" (閼伽香水, Aka Kōsui).
In ancient India, it was customary to offer water for washing feet and rinsing the mouth after meals to guests. This practice was adopted in {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|qid=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}} and became a part of offerings made before Buddha statues or to monks.
The well from which argha is drawn is called "Argha Well" (閼伽井), and the structure covering it is known as "Argha Well House" (閼伽井屋) or "Argha Well Hall" (閼伽井堂). The bottle used to store argha is called a "Water Bottle" (水瓶, Suibyō) or "Kundi" (軍持). The vessel used to contain argha is called an "Argha Vessel" (閼伽器), and the shelf for offering argha is referred to as an "Argha Shelf" (閼伽棚).
In addition to water, offerings on the argha shelf may include flowers, ferns, autumn leaves, nuts, and other items.<ref>Comprehensive Ancient Language Dictionary, Publisher: Obunsha, p.26, mentions offerings of water and flowers.</ref><ref>Beginners Classics: Japanese Classics Hōjōki (Complete), p.124, Kadokawa Corporation, describes offerings before Buddha statues and includes an earlier mention of crafting an argha shelf on p.124.</ref>
== References ==
